insight: Shredder and Hannibull are most efficient when opponent cannot kill them at exact absorb thresholds; buying Shredder early before opponent can kill it is less viable than Hannibull due to tempo loss.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of frontline unit value and absorb mechanics.

insight: If opponent's blocking Shredder or Hannibull can be killed with available damage, always kill it because opponent otherwise gets to choose whether to lose it, removing their strategic flexibility.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of when to pressure vs preserve enemy frontline.

insight: Xaetron as sole defender is not sufficient justification for 3rd Engineer opening; early Conduit is required to accelerate Xaetron but limits early defense against rushes.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of when to take 3rd Engineer based on available defenses.

insight: Xaetron should not be held back on defense every turn; defending with it every 3rd turn wastes heal value and is rarely worth it except in rare circumstances like against Wincer.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of Xaetron defensive patterns and value.

insight: Xaetron's primary value is absorbing blue resources efficiently; buying multiple Infusion Grids as alternative is cost-inefficient compared to single Xaetron.
author: cachotognax
context: Discussion of Xaetron versus Infusion Grid as absorbers.

insight: Soak means sacrificing a unit on defense to absorb damage; frontline units like Shredder, Hannibull, and Polywall soak efficiently when opponent cannot attack for exact amounts.
author: amalloy
context: Clarification of soak vs absorb terminology.

insight: Shredder provides 1 threat plus absorb denial capability; it is frontline-based soak similar to Infusion Grid but with threat value, making it better when backline absorber is already established.
author: amalloy
context: Comparing Shredder and Infusion Grid as soak options.

insight: Redeemer is effective in both high and low inflation scenarios; it underperforms in mid-inflation situations where neither player is pressuring heavily.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of Redeemer inflation dependency.

insight: Buying Redeemer is strategically sound when opponent is about to deploy powerful charged attackers (e.g., Gauss Cannon with multiple charges) that will burst next turn.
author: ravnabergsndot
context: Discussion of Redeemer timing and counterplay.

insight: Early frontline units like Shredder allow opponent to deny absorb; in slow sets, getting drones and better soak options (Bombarder, Aegis, Gauss Cannon) is preferable.
author: medar
context: Replay analysis of slow-set economy versus frontline defense strategy.

insight: Holding 1 Scorchilla per absorb denied is standard rule of thumb; holding more wastes damage unless denying multiple specific absorbs.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of Scorchilla holding strategy versus Plexo Cell.

insight: Spending significant health/tempo to deal 1 damage with frontline units (e.g., 7 HP of Hannibull for 1 damage) is inefficient; attack only if killing valuable targets or severely overdefended.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of frontline unit attack efficiency.

insight: Being down drones against Master Bot is often correct because the bot overbuild drones; winning despite drone deficit indicates better tech and attack decisions.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of drone economy versus bot play patterns.

insight: DD/DD/DAB is a strong Hellhound opener that minimizes gold float; it is preferable to alternatives that float 3+ gold in sets with aggressive attackers.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of optimized Hellhound opening sequences.

insight: In sets with Bloodrager and Vai, 3rd Engineer is viable when strong absorbers like Infusion Grid are available; Bloodrager makes it close but Vai's delayed access to P2 and drones needed for it balances the pressure.
author: cogito3
context: Analysis of 3rd Engineer viability against aggressive red units.

insight: Holding attack (e.g., Immolites or Iso Kronus) to synchronize with Scorchillas does not increase pressure unless denying absorb; opponent rebuilds attack instead of defense during held turns.
author: jonshea
context: Discussion of why holding damage for synchronization is suboptimal.

insight: Synchronized burst damage can be effective against weaker players who fail to preemptively build defense; stronger players will rebuild defense efficiently so synced damage provides little advantage unless denying absorb.
author: flameberger
context: Discussion of synced attack effectiveness by skill level.

insight: Prompt blockers like Wall and Infusion Grid are more efficient when needed each turn; buying defense preemptively before synced damage reduces their value since threat disappears after burst.
author: cogito3
context: Discussion of why burst damage is strategically superior to synced damage.

insight: Against Zemora Voidbringer, P1 should not open DD/DC unless Zemora is the only strong attacker; P1 DD/DA or similar pressure openings are superior because Zemora requires heavy Conduit investment.
author: extratricky
context: Discussion of anti-Zemora opening strategy.

insight: Against Zemora, if P2 opens DC/DC early, the resulting Zemora is too slow compared to P1 response, especially since P2 cannot maintain economy for defense.
author: consensus
context: Discussion of Zemora opening optimization.

insight: With Zemora, do not accumulate excessive green tech (more than 10 conduits) before Zemora is active; use Zemora's gold generation to build remaining Conduits over subsequent turns.
author: cogito3
context: Replay analysis of Zemora economy mistakes.

insight: Apollo is weak unless sniping high-value targets (Vivid Drone, Lucina) that it can prevent; sniping Tarsier or Wall provides minimal value due to Apollo's cost and buildtime.
author: namington
context: Discussion of Apollo effectiveness and target selection.

insight: Apollo can be good when opponent's other attackers (e.g., Lucina) would be absorbed on first hit; committing to Lucina after opponent commits to Apollo creates favorable positioning.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion of Lucina vs Apollo buildup sequencing.

insight: Centurion is cost-efficient against Shiver Yeti; if opponent buys 3 Shiver Yeti to deny Centurion, you spent less tech (18GG BBR) for more permanent attack (2 more damage per turn) than opponent (15RRR).
author: f300xen
context: Discussion of Centurion viability in Shiver Yeti sets.

insight: In Centurion + Xeno sets, 3 Shiver Yeti only denies 2 damage (not 3) since Xeno is not frozen; Shiver Yeti is weak in this configuration.
author: supergreyman
context: Discussion of Shiver Yeti vs Xenophage interaction.

insight: With 1-Stamina Bombarder, holding it (not clicking) is usually correct because clicking spends threat equal to damage dealt; absorbing for 3 maintains threat without losing efficiency.
author: cogito3
context: Discussion of Bombarder stamina economics and threat value.

insight: 1-Stamina Bombarder exceptions: click it to kill valuable non-defense units (Rhino, Drone) or to set up soaking next turn; 2-Stamina Bombarder should almost always be clicked.
author: cogito3
context: Discussion of Bombarder stamina strategy exceptions.

insight: Mega Drone enables building attack early in red-heavy sets; absorbing on Mega Drone wastes 4 gold per turn but denies opponent's absorption-denial strategy.
author: torikunsnaps
context: Discussion of Mega Drone's role in red rush prevention.

insight: High-economy openings (e.g., DD/DD/DDE/DDD/DDDB into red) justify drone-heavy early builds with blue tech when good absorbers are present to defend against early aggression.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion of high-economy opening requirements.

insight: Against aggressive Tarsier-heavy defense, high-economy players should prioritize stronger absorbers (Centurion, Doomed Wall, Aegis) over walls; without these, walls alone cannot stop repeated Tarsier spam.
author: terroking
context: Discussion of high-economy defense against aggressive rushes.

insight: Shock units (Plasmafier, Drake, Grenade Mech, Tesla Coil) can deter early rushes by threatening burst damage, forcing opponent to slow down even if they do not deal consistent damage.
author: swapgo
context: Discussion of defensive units against early aggression.
